full_response:
Let  

 J = { j , j+1 , … , j+k−1 }                  (the positions that form the candidate block)  
 L = j−1 (mod N)                            (the seat immediately before J)  
 R = j+k       (mod N)                      (the seat immediately after J)

In the final seating arrangement J is a block of length k starting at j  
iff

 • every seat in J is occupied, and  
 • the two neighbouring seats L and R are empty.

Let B_{j,k} be this event; by definition p_{j,k}=Pr[B_{j,k}].  
Let E_{j,k} be the event that at least k of the n initially assigned
random seats lie in J.  We must show

                       Pr[B_{j,k}] ≤ Pr[E_{j,k}].       (1)

In other words, if the block B_{j,k} occurs, then necessarily E_{j,k} occurs.
We prove this by contradiction.

-------------------------------------------------
Claim.  Assume that in the final configuration B_{j,k} happens.
Then every guest who finally sits in J originally received a random
seat inside J.  Consequently at least k random seats fall in J, i.e. E_{j,k} holds.
-------------------------------------------------
Proof (by contradiction).

Pick an arbitrary seat s∈J and let g be the guest who finally occupies s.
Suppose, for contradiction, that g’s random seat r_g is not in J.

Case 1   r_g lies clockwise before j, i.e. r_g,…,L ,J in that order.

 When g arrived, she started at r_g and walked clockwise until she found
 the first empty seat.  
 Seat L is empty in the final configuration, hence no one ever sat there;
 therefore L was empty when g came by.  
 Because L appears before s on g’s walk, g would have stopped at L,
 contradicting the fact that she finally occupies s∈J.

Case 2   r_g lies clockwise after R, i.e. J ,R ,…,r_g in that order.

 Now R is empty in the final configuration, so again it has been empty
 throughout the whole process.  
 Since R lies between r_g and s on g’s clockwise walk, g would have
 stopped at R, contradicting that she reaches s∈J.

Both cases yield contradictions, hence our assumption is impossible and
r_g must belong to J.  Because the k seats of J are all occupied, we have
found at least k distinct guests whose random seats lie in J, i.e. E_{j,k}
occurs.  Thus B_{j,k} ⇒ E_{j,k}.
∎

Since B_{j,k} implies E_{j,k}, the probabilities satisfy (1):

                       p_{j,k}=Pr[B_{j,k}] ≤ Pr[E_{j,k}].

-------------------------------------------------
Therefore

\boxed{\,p_{j,k}\;\le\;\Pr[E_{j,k}]\,}

-------------------------------------------------
Confidence: 75%
